sentences.text: Yeah.
From there, we went to Stanislav, Tarnopol, Stanislav, Tarnopol, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and [NON-ENGLISH].
And then from there, we went to Lemberg.
Three days, four days.
We were always they had a place where to stay where they put us in.
So sometimes-- excuse me, midnight, 12:00 o'clock, car loads of guns come in.
We are unloaded on the trucks.
And they took it.
And sometimes, we get from the trucks were loaded back to the train, and took it.
A few days, three, four days.
Now, after that we went to Lemberg.
That's the capital of Pole-- not Pole-- Galicia.
There, they everybody-- they asked all 228 of us, what you do for a living?
Somebody says, I'm a doctor.
He went there.
I'm a engineer.
He went there.
I write books.
I'm a songwriter, or this.
I'm a butcher.
I'm a shoemaker.
I'm a tailor.
He says, you know how to clean the latrine.
I says, yes, I know how to clean it.
Never done it in my life.
I almost fainted.
By that time, officers were there.
Lebal was there.
His partner was a Jew.
Lebal, Lebal, Lebal, Lebal-- L-E-B-A-L.
He was a-- he was a captain.
In the Hungarian army.
He was a very, very nice.
So they ask everybody, what is it?
So I says, I'm a farmer.
So he says, you know how to clean?
I says, yes, Captain, I would like to do that.
So right away, I got a clean, you know, uniform.
And then I-- I cook-- I-- I didn't cook for him.
But they had a different kitchen.
The officers-- there was 1,000 workers there in Lemberg there, in the big beer factory-- over 1,000 workers, 108 [INAUDIBLE], 108 [INAUDIBLE].
And they had their own kitchen.
And one day, he says, you know, George-- I says, [INAUDIBLE].
And then there was the warehouse, you know.
We were-- I worked in the warehouse.
And I-- and I cleaned his-- his room, and everything.
And one day, he says to me, I don't know what the hell I'm going to do.
He says, I'm getting six German officers here.
I says, leave it to me.
I'm going to make you something good.
Somewhere I got there in the village were three, four chickens.
They killed them.
And I took, you know, the giblet, the head, and everything out, and the-- and the feet, and cleaned it, and got fresh green peas.
And then I got some way, a spring pan.
And I made blintzes, seven or eight blintzes.
I had one blintzes, one walnuts, cooked it up, this and that, put it back in the oven.
And at night, we took it out.
I says, I can't go.
You must ask my captain.
So every week, three times I was baking for the Germans, always different-- different kind of food.
The Germans was all there too.
The Germans-- they were watching us, the officers, you know?
And a couple of German soldiers, Sergeant, this, that.
And I was very popular when I made them excellent-- I made them food they never ate in their life.
I says, you know, Captain, you could really do me a favor.
[NON-ENGLISH] a picture is playing [NON-ENGLISH] in Lemberg.
I would like to see that picture.
And I would like to bring you officers some good pastries from the-- they bake excellent-- excellent pastries in Lemberg there.
So I got a passport.
I got a passport with a white band that I got a right to go to the movie and buy pastries.
But when I go to the movie, it was written, [GERMAN], just for German soldiers.
My God, I coming to see the movie.
Every morning, they give us a black coffee and a piece of black bread.
And 12 people in the truck-- this was January or February.
And how we were going, going, going up, the truck hardly could go.
Then they put us back in the truck.
They called up 10 people, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, And they called two of us up.
And we had to bury them.
That went 10 days.
In Lemberg.
In the Jewish-- where was the truck?
We were taking to the sanitarium there-- it wasn't a sanitarium.
There was a Jewish sanitarium there in Lemberg.
And we had to dig them.
And then, you know, they send us back to the car.
That was-- God was with me twice in my life.
[INAUDIBLE] They sent back all of us.
They called up ten.
They shot them.
We had to bury them.
The next day, we went back.
That went for 10 days there.
It was left over, 10 of us.
And we said, some way, now, we're going to get-- we're going to die.
They don't want us no more.
He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I fainted-- excuse me-- I shit all over-- From the faint, and from the fear.
[PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] They went out.
They wash me up.
They took me out of there.
They took me a bath.
They shave me.
For three days, I didn't even have to go to work.
When they came, you know, they said I'm sick.
I got to go to the hospital.
They need me.
We're working for the German Wehrmacht.
That's what I told you.
They didn't kill us.
The German Wehrmacht did not kill us.
We work for them.
They needed us for transferring this, and transferring that.
And then the Russians was coming, pushing, pushing.
After that, I went back there.
We were coming-- we were in [NON-ENGLISH].
That was Czechoslovakia that time.
And then the most of them, they took to Auschwitz and Buchenwald.
But I was-- I was not that sick that they take me to the gas chamber.
I don't know they took them all to the gas chamber or not.
So they said that I-- that I got to go to the hospital.
We went back to Bratislava.
That was the capital of-- now is the capital of Slovakia.
That time, it belongs to Czechoslovakia.
Before that, was Hungary.
And it was Poczajow.
From that 200, about 120 had [GERMAN].
They took us up in a separate room.
But they didn't know I was Jewish.
Just my name was there.
I was working for the German Wehrmacht.
And then they took us up in the room.
And these young kids 16, 17, 18 years, they were dying with flies.
One day, an older-- he was a doctor.
He was older.
And he sees my name Geza Riechfeld.
He says, your father Eddie.
[INAUDIBLE] I'm gone.
He says, don't worry.
I'm going to save your life.
Your father was my friend.
They took them to Auschwitz and Buchenwald.
So we went way further.
Then we went to Ruhnu, Duvno, Kremnitz, [INAUDIBLE], almost to Stalingrad.
No, it was German.
It was Hungarian, and all kinds of national was-- was in that.
There was-- most of them was Pollock, Polish people.
It was-- it was still 117.
That was the-- the number of the whole unit, 101 by 17.
Yeah, that was formed in [NON-ENGLISH] where we went in.
No, no, that was mixed.

And then in the meantime, they give the people in there was Jews, Catholic, everything who was half Jewish, or they had a girlfriend or-- or-- or-- or making false checks, you know, they-- they weren't clean.
There was somebody named [NON-ENGLISH].
He was a Jewish fellow.
He killed his wife when they want to separate them, [NON-ENGLISH].
He was there.
But then he got killed.
And then we went almost-- almost about 100-- we went to Ruhnu, Duvno--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Oh, almost a year.
It's gotta be '43, '44.
Yes.
Yes, yes, we know it.
We got together with the-- with the-- with the people.
I never was in the ghetto.
I never heard about a ghetto, except when the war was over.
We were out of the city where the big beer factory was in Lemberg.
We never stayed in the city inside.
Yeah, that was in Slovakia, in [NON-ENGLISH].
I told you that.
And there where they took me in the hospital.
One of the soldiers, they just pushed me down from the fifth floor.
They were emptying the warehouses, you know?
And I fall in something.
And the rest of them, you know, they had to go to Auschwitz.
But they thought I be ready pretty soon for work.
And they needed the workers there.
And that's why I went to-- to Bratislava to the-- to the hospital.
Yeah, we went to Ruhnu, Duvno, Kremnitz, [INAUDIBLE].
All-- no in Kremnitz we-- in-- in Duvno we stayed a half a year--
--chopping woods and this and that.
The same thing, little villages, chopping woods, and bringing them in, bringing in, and unloading the carloads of stuff.
And a few things happen when we were in-- in-- in-- in Kremnitz.
I says, you know, you can have it.
Go in the warehouse.
Give to me.
I do the same thing what I do to them.
Bring in the village.
They give you this.
They bring you that.
And then the-- three German officers come in to check-- I told you I worked in the warehouse too there.
They come in to check the warehouse, missing this, missing that, missing that.
So somebody said one of us-- the Jews must steal it.
They said, they couldn't steal so much.
By that time?
Beginning, it was 228.
From-- from-- from when I was?
I don't think there was 10, 12.
There was all kind of nationals there.
Yes, there was Polish Jews, Russian Jews.
We find them there.
Just when I was in the [INAUDIBLE] in Lemberg, there was Polish Jews, Russian Jews, all kinds of Jews there.
Some of them prayed.
Some of them didn't.
I never I-- I-- I said my pray at night.
I told you what happened when we were in-- in-- in Kremnitz, Lebal asked somebody who don't want to work that day who wants to fast can have a day off.
And then-- and then when he went up to the-- everybody was eating.
We didn't have a day off then, I think, for three months or three weeks.
I don't remember.
He was decent to us who don't want to work for Yom Kippur, can have that day off.
He was very nice.
He was-- he was a prince.
Lebal, the captain.
He saved my life twice, that he teach-- that I cooked for him, you know.
He was the person who I cooked when he invited the German soldiers there.
He was a wonderful-- I told you, I never saw a German killing a Jew.
[INAUDIBLE] excuse me.
[INAUDIBLE].
Oh, by the beginning, at least 100 escaped to the parties as they thought if they escaped to the [? partisans ?], especially in Kremnitz and Ruhnu and [NON-ENGLISH].
More than half escaped to the [? partisans. ?] And we didn't even see one come back alive.
We-- we-- we heard rumors that a lot of them escaped.
A lot of them, don't forget, was from the [NON-ENGLISH].
They talked perfect Russian, you know.
They thought that they escaped.
But we never-- not one come back of those with over 100, not even one.
No, I tell you what we heard rumors.
The guards used to get letters that were just a card, we're fine, and hope you are fine.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't get no letters.
We had a postcard.
We're fine.
Hope you are fine.
We're fine.
That's all.
Everything was printed, no writing.
I never heard about the concentration camp along the war wasn't over.
No, ma'am.
The forced labor-- I want to tell you one thing.
We had plenty food.
We worked for the [INAUDIBLE], for the Wehrmacht.
The German had the best food in the world.
You have no idea what food they had.
They had everything, what they-- all from-- all from Holland, Belgium, Denmark, Yugoslavia, Hungary, from all over the world what they stoled, the Germans had the best food in the world.
They weren't hungry.
They had everything in the world.
Every night, they had six hot meals every week, except Saturday.
Sunday, they had no hot meals-- cold cuts.
Yes.
There was, we had a dentist.
We had a doctor.
Doctor's name was Dr.
Mandel.
And I got to tell you a little story of him.
He's my best friend, Dr.
Mandel.
He was from Budapest.
He was a dentist.
But there, he got to be a doctor.
So I had my friend [NON-ENGLISH], I told you is the only one left alive I know.
He took him.
He took him that he's sick.
He's got to take him to the hospital.
In the meantime, both talk perfect Russians.
And some way, they got into the [? partisan ?] village, these two Dr.
Mandel and [NON-ENGLISH].
And both talk perfect Russian.
And when the war was over, even after the war, two years later, the captain of one of these DP-- not DP-- I don't know what camp-- was a-- a-- a doctor, a Jewish doctor.
Labor.
You will be-- sometimes, you work 20 hours a day.
We unloaded them.
We load it back, and this and that.
But food, we had very good-- we had good food.
Only time I served a cook when we got to Lemberg.
After, I says, I'm a farmer, you know?
But I work in the warehouse too.
And then I had to go here.
I got to go there.
But my life was pretty good. 12 of us went every day to the warehouse to load.
But then, if we come in car loads, then we had to get 20, 30 people more to help us to work.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
After that, we were about 160 miles from Stalingrad.
And then we heard the news.
We read first in the paper that-- that the German U-boats cannot sink so much boats what America cannot rebuild, and that, how long is the war going to be?
The Hungarian-- maybe you-- did you heard that the Hungarians, they put down the guns.
I don't know.
That was in 1944 in January.
They put down the guns.
They don't want to fight no more.
But they pushed us back.
No, I want to tell you how we were coming back, you never saw so much clothes, and so much the Germans [INAUDIBLE] take what you want.
But they were afraid.
They tried to throw away their uniform and get regular private uniforms.
And then they pushed us back.
And I know I had a friend.
His name was Imre Ehrenwald.
His originally name was Ehrenwald.
But he played in the Hungarian national team, very good soccer play.
You couldn't have a Jewish name.
So his name was Agri.
And when we went-- when we come back through the-- the-- the-- when you-- what the hell-- the [INAUDIBLE], when you go through the--
The border.
We start to cry.
He says, you-- you animal.
What you crying for?
Didn't they do enough stuff for you?
And then he said, he's going home.
And then they could-- didn't let us go.
And I told us that Sargent Grunfelder who was very nice to us during the war.
Then after the war, we bought him a bookstore in Lipotvaros.
That was the biggest Jewish section there in Budapest, Lipotvaros.
We bought him a bookstore.
He was wonderful to us.
By the frost, yea.
They start behaving much different after-- after, say, we were two thirds back from Stalingrad, they were different persons.
They know they lost the war.
Everybody wanted a good word.
In Bratislava, I told you.
Didn't you mark that up in Bratislava?
Yes.
That was in Bratislava.
In Bratislava.
That's [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the capital of Slovakia now.
And some way, the Germans know it.
And they took them-- that was Christmas day, 1944, Christmas Day.
They took them.
That was the worst thing I ever saw in my life, two to 10 years old kids.
The Germans, but we didn't see that.
I said I never saw a-- a German killing a Jew.
That was the worst thing in the world, and one more.
So we were coming back.
The Germans tried to get private clothes.
They give everything, you know, just to have a different clothes not in German uniform.
We had to go with them ahead of them.
We had to pack up the stuff what was in there.
They, you know-- the Swiss cheese was 4-- 500 pounds.
Everything in the world, the best cocoa, the best chocolate, the best booze, everything.
The Germans had everything the best, what they stole from all the small countries.
Move-- Yeah.
Oh, there were thousands, motor bicycles, and-- and big trucks, and everything.
